book.name: Micah
book.author: Micah of Moresheth
book.writtenFrom: Moresheth-gath, a village in the Judean foothills
book.writtenTo: The people of Judah and Israel

explainer.overview: Micah is a rural prophet from the Judean foothills who confronts the urban corruption of both Samaria and Jerusalem. He passionately defends the poor against exploitation by powerful landowners, corrupt judges, and false prophets. Yet Micah also delivers some of the most beloved promises in Scripture: the birth of the Messiah in Bethlehem (5:2), the vision of swords beaten into plowshares (4:3), and the summary of true religion as doing justice, loving mercy, and walking humbly with God (6:8).
explainer.historicalContext: Micah prophesied during the reigns of Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah -- overlapping with Isaiah but from a very different social location. While Isaiah operated in the royal court, Micah was a small-town villager who saw firsthand how the powerful exploited rural communities. He witnessed the fall of Samaria to Assyria (722 BC) and warned that Jerusalem would face the same fate.
explainer.whyItMatters: Micah gives voice to the voiceless -- the rural poor crushed by urban elites. His prophecy that the Messiah would come from tiny Bethlehem rather than mighty Jerusalem embodies his theology: God works through the small and humble, not the powerful and prestigious. His summary of true religion in 6:8 remains the most concise and beloved ethical statement in the Old Testament.
keyThemes: Social justice for the poor and vulnerable
Swords into plowshares -- the vision of peace

people.name: Micah of Moresheth
people.bio: Micah came from Moresheth-gath, a small agricultural village in the Shephelah (foothills) about 25 miles southwest of Jerusalem. As a rural villager, he witnessed how wealthy urban elites seized peasant lands and exploited the poor. His prophecy is driven by passionate concern for economic justice and authentic worship.
people.culturalContext: Micah represents the perspective of the rural poor in a time when wealthy landowners were consolidating property and displacing small farmers -- a process condemned throughout the prophets.

places.name: Moresheth-gath
places.description: A small village in the Judean foothills (Shephelah) about 25 miles southwest of Jerusalem, near the Philistine city of Gath.
places.culturalSignificance: Moresheth was in the agricultural heartland of Judah, vulnerable to both Assyrian invasion from the north and Philistine pressure from the west. Micah's rural perspective shaped his concern for small farmers and the vulnerable.

places.name: Bethlehem Ephrathah
places.description: A small town about 5 miles south of Jerusalem, from which the Messiah would come according to Micah 5:2.
places.culturalSignificance: Bethlehem was David's hometown, and Micah prophesied that the future ruler of Israel would also come from this insignificant village -- emphasizing God's preference for the humble and small over the powerful and great.

timeline.event: Micah begins prophesying during Jotham's reign
Reign of Ahaz -- spiritual decline and Assyrian pressure
Fall of Samaria to Assyria -- Micah had warned this would happen
Reign of Hezekiah -- Micah's prophecy influences reform (Jeremiah 26:18)
Sennacherib's invasion of Judah devastates the Shephelah region near Micah's home

explainer.summary: God comes from His holy temple to judge Samaria and Jerusalem. Samaria's sins are terminal -- the city will become a heap of ruins. Micah then laments the coming destruction through a series of wordplays on the names of towns in his home region.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Elevated worship sites often associated with Canaa
wordStudies.meaning: Elevated worship sites often associated with Canaanite religion
wordStudies.significance: The 'high places' represented the syncretism of Israel's worship -- blending Yahweh worship with Canaanite practices at outdoor hilltop shrines.

verses.text: Yahweh’s word that came to Micah the Morashtite in the days of Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah, kings of Judah, which he saw concerning Samaria and Jerusalem.
verses.explainer: The heading places Micah in the reigns of three Judean kings (Jotham, Ahaz, Hezekiah) -- spanning roughly 750-700 BC. He is from Moresheth, a small town in the Judean lowlands, and his message concerns both Samaria (northern Israel) and Jerusalem (southern Judah).

verses.text:
Hear, you peoples, all of you.
Listen, O earth, and all that is therein:
and let the Lord Yahweh be witness against you,
the Lord from his holy temple.
verses.explainer: Micah summons all peoples of the earth to listen as God Himself takes the witness stand against them. The heavenly temple becomes a courtroom, and God is both plaintiff and judge.

annotations.word: Lord Yahweh be witness against you

verses.text:
For, behold, Yahweh comes out of his place,
and will come down and tread on the high places of the earth.
verses.explainer: God comes out from His heavenly dwelling and descends to earth, treading on the mountain peaks. This theophany -- God appearing in power -- echoes ancient descriptions of divine warriors marching to battle.

crossReferences.reference: Amos 4:13 -- 'He who treads on the heights of the earth -- Yahweh, God of Armies, is his name'
crossReferences.connection: Amos 4:13 -- 'He who treads on the heights of the earth -- Yahweh, God of Armies, is his name'

verses.text:
The mountains melt under him,
and the valleys split apart,
like wax before the fire,
like waters that are poured down a steep place.
verses.explainer: Mountains melt under God's feet like wax near fire; valleys split apart like water cascading down a slope. The natural world cannot withstand God's presence -- even the most permanent features of the landscape dissolve.

verses.text:
“All this is for the disobedience of Jacob,
and for the sins of the house of Israel.
What is the disobedience of Jacob?
Isn’t it Samaria?
And what are the high places of Judah?
Aren’t they Jerusalem?
verses.explainer: The cause of this cosmic upheaval: Jacob's rebellion and Israel's sins. Samaria is identified as the epicenter of northern Israel's sin, and Jerusalem as the 'high place' of Judah's corruption. Both capitals are guilty.

verses.text:
Therefore I will make Samaria like a rubble heap of the field,
like places for planting vineyards;
and I will pour down its stones into the valley,
and I will uncover its foundations.
verses.explainer: God will reduce Samaria to rubble -- a heap of stones in a field, suitable only for planting vineyards. The grand capital of northern Israel will be demolished down to its foundations, its stones poured into the valley.

Samaria's idols will be smashed, her temple gifts burned, her images destroyed. Since these were acquired through spiritual prostitution (worshiping foreign gods for material gain), they will return to that same purpose -- the wealth that funded idolatry will fund other nations' temples.

Micah laments -- stripping naked and howling like a jackal, moaning like an ostrich. The prophet himself acts out the grief of judgment. His emotional response shows that true prophets do not enjoy delivering bad news.

verses.text:
For her wounds are incurable;
for it has come even to Judah.
It reaches to the gate of my people,
even to Jerusalem.
verses.explainer: Samaria's wounds are incurable, and the infection has spread south to Judah, reaching the very gate of Jerusalem. What began in the north is coming for the south. The judgment is contagious and unstoppable.

verses.text:
Don’t tell it in Gath.
Don’t weep at all.
At Beth Ophrah I have rolled myself in the dust.
verses.explainer: A series of wordplays on town names begins: 'Don't tell it in Gath' (echoing David's lament in 2 Samuel 1:20). At Beth Ophrah ('house of dust'), Micah rolls in the dust. Each town's name becomes a prophetic pun about its fate.

verses.text:
Pass on, inhabitant of Shaphir, in nakedness and shame.
The inhabitant of Zaanan won’t come out.
The wailing of Beth Ezel will take from you his protection.
verses.explainer: More wordplays: Shaphir ('beautiful town') will pass away in shameful nakedness. Zaanan ('going out') will not go out. Beth Ezel ('nearby house') will be unable to provide nearby support. Each town's name ironically describes its coming destruction.

verses.text:
For the inhabitant of Maroth waits anxiously for good,
because evil has come down from Yahweh to the gate of Jerusalem.
verses.explainer: Maroth ('bitterness') waits anxiously for good news, but disaster has come from Yahweh to Jerusalem's gate. The name wordplay continues -- the 'bitter town' receives bitter news.

Lachish ('horse team') is told to harness chariots -- the city known for its military horses was the beginning of sin for Zion. Lachish, the second most important city in Judah, imported northern Israel's sins into the south.

annotations.word: beginning of sin to the daughter of Zion

verses.text:
Therefore you will give a parting gift to Moresheth Gath.
The houses of Achzib will be a deceitful thing to the kings of Israel.
verses.explainer: Moresheth-Gath (Micah's own hometown) will receive parting gifts -- like a bride's dowry, the town is being sent away. Achzib ('deception') will be a deception to Israel's kings. The prophet's own community is not spared.

verses.text:
I will yet bring to you, inhabitant of Mareshah.
He who is the glory of Israel will come to Adullam.
verses.explainer: Mareshah ('possession') will be taken as a possession by a conqueror. Israel's glory will retreat to Adullam -- the cave where David hid as a fugitive. The nation is reduced from royalty to refugee status.

verses.text:
Shave your heads,
and cut off your hair for the children of your delight.
Enlarge your baldness like the vulture;
for they have gone into captivity from you!
verses.explainer: Shave your heads in mourning for your beloved children, because they are going into exile. Enlarge your baldness like a vulture -- the bald-headed vulture becomes the image of a nation stripped bare by loss.

explainer.summary: Woe to those who plan iniquity on their beds and carry it out at dawn -- especially land grabbers who seize fields and houses. God announces disaster against these oppressors. False prophets who promise prosperity are condemned.

wordStudies.significance: The powerful 'devise' evil at night and execute it at dawn -- their sin is premeditated, systematic, and leverages their power advantage.

verses.text:
Woe to those who devise iniquity
and work evil on their beds!
When the morning is light, they practice it,
because it is in the power of their hand.
verses.explainer: Woe to those who lie awake at night planning wickedness, then carry it out at first light simply because they have the power to do so. Their evil is premeditated and power-driven -- not impulsive but calculated.

thenVsNow.then: Systems of oppression are rarely spontaneous -- they are planned, designed, and executed by those who have the power to implement them. Micah indicts not just wrongdoing but the deliberate architecture of injustice.
thenVsNow.now: Systems of oppression are rarely spontaneous -- they are planned, designed, and executed by those who have the power to implement them. Micah indicts not just wrongdoing but the deliberate architecture of injustice.

verses.text:
They covet fields, and seize them;
and houses, and take them away:
and they oppress a man and his house,
even a man and his heritage.
verses.explainer: They covet fields and seize them, houses and take them. They defraud a man of his home, a family of its inheritance. The powerful are stealing land from ordinary people -- the very inheritance God gave each family in the Promised Land.

crossReferences.reference: 1 Kings 21 -- Ahab and Jezebel's seizure of Naboth's vineyard is the classic example of exactly what Micah describes
crossReferences.connection: 1 Kings 21 -- Ahab and Jezebel's seizure of Naboth's vineyard is the classic example of exactly what Micah describes

verses.explainer: Therefore God is planning disaster against these land-grabbers -- a yoke they cannot remove from their necks. They will no longer walk proudly, because the time will be evil. God matches their scheming with His own planning.

verses.text:
In that day they will take up a parable against you,
and lament with a doleful lamentation, saying,
‘We are utterly ruined!
My people’s possession is divided up.
Indeed he takes it from me and assigns our fields to traitors!’”
verses.explainer: On that day, people will taunt the oppressors with a mocking song: 'We are utterly ruined! He changes the portion of my people!' The land-grabbers will lose their own land -- the same fate they inflicted on others.

verses.text: Therefore you will have no one who divides the land by lot in Yahweh’s assembly.
verses.explainer: The land-grabbers will have no one to divide land for them in Yahweh's assembly. They will be excluded from the future redistribution of the Promised Land -- permanent exile from the community of God's people.

verses.text:
“Don’t prophesy!”
They prophesy.
“Don’t prophesy about these things.
Disgrace won’t overtake us.”
verses.explainer: The people try to silence the prophets: 'Don't prophesy! Don't speak about these things! Disgrace won't overtake us!' They want to hear only comfortable messages and reject any word of warning or accountability.

verses.text:
Shall it be said, O house of Jacob:
“Is Yahweh’s Spirit angry?
Are these his doings?
Don’t my words do good to him who walks blamelessly?”
verses.explainer: The people question whether God's Spirit could really be angry: 'Are these really His actions?' But God responds: His words do good to those who walk uprightly. The problem is not God's anger but the people's behavior.

verses.text:
But lately my people have risen up as an enemy.
You strip the robe and clothing from those who pass by without a care, returning from battle.
verses.explainer: God's people have become the enemy: they strip the robe from peaceful passersby, making those who feel safe into war refugees. The powerful exploit even those who mean no harm and expect no danger.

verses.text:
You drive the women of my people out from their pleasant houses;
from their young children you take away my blessing forever.
verses.explainer: They drive women from their pleasant homes and take away God's blessing from their children forever. The oppression targets the most vulnerable -- women and children who lose their security and their future.

verses.text:
Arise, and depart!
For this is not your resting place,
because of uncleanness that destroys,
even with a grievous destruction.
verses.explainer: Get up and leave! This is no longer your resting place. Because of the people's uncleanness, the land itself will destroy them. The Promised Land, meant to be Israel's 'rest,' rejects those who defile it.

crossReferences.reference: Leviticus 18:28 -- The land will 'vomit you out' if you defile it, as it vomited out the nations before you
crossReferences.connection: Leviticus 18:28 -- The land will 'vomit you out' if you defile it, as it vomited out the nations before you

verses.explainer: If a man walking in falsehood says 'I will prophesy to you of wine and strong drink,' he would be the perfect prophet for this people! They want preachers who tell them what they want to hear -- that indulgence is fine.

thenVsNow.then: The desire for prophets who preach comfort rather than truth is timeless. People consistently prefer messages that affirm their choices over messages that call for change.
thenVsNow.now: The desire for prophets who preach comfort rather than truth is timeless. People consistently prefer messages that affirm their choices over messages that call for change.

verses.explainer: A sudden shift to hope: God will surely assemble all of Jacob, gather the remnant of Israel together. He will put them together like sheep in a pen, like a flock in its pasture -- a noisy multitude of people.

verses.text:
He who breaks open the way goes up before them.
They break through the gate, and go out.
And their king passes on before them,
with Yahweh at their head.
verses.explainer: The one who breaks open the way goes before them. They break through the gate and go out. Their king passes before them -- Yahweh at their head. God Himself leads the breakout from captivity like a king leading his people to freedom.

explainer.summary: Leaders who should know justice instead eat the flesh of God's people. Prophets who prophesy for money are condemned. Zion will be plowed as a field because of corrupt leadership -- a prophecy so powerful it was quoted 100 years later to save Jeremiah's life.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Justice, judgment, the right ordering of society
wordStudies.meaning: Justice, judgment, the right ordering of society
wordStudies.significance: Micah uses 'mishpat' to contrast what leaders should do with what they actually do. They should 'know justice' but instead they 'hate good and love evil.'

verses.text:
I said,
“Please listen, you heads of Jacob,
and rulers of the house of Israel:
Isn’t it for you to know justice?
verses.explainer: Micah addresses the rulers directly: You of all people should know justice! Leaders are not excused from moral knowledge -- they are the ones most responsible for understanding and implementing what is right.

Instead, the leaders love evil and hate good. They tear the skin off God's people and the flesh from their bones. The metaphor is deliberately gruesome -- the rulers are butchers who devour the people they should protect.

verses.text:
who also eat the flesh of my people,
and flay their skin from off them,
and break their bones,
and chop them in pieces, as for the pot,
and as meat within the cauldron.
verses.explainer: They eat the people's flesh, flay their skin, break their bones, and chop them up like meat for the pot. Micah extends the cannibalism metaphor to its horrifying conclusion: the rulers treat their own people as livestock.

verses.text:
Then they will cry to Yahweh,
but he will not answer them.
Yes, he will hide his face from them at that time,
because they made their deeds evil.”
verses.explainer: When disaster comes, these rulers will cry to God for help -- but He will not answer. He will hide His face from them because they practiced evil. Those who ignored the cries of the oppressed will find their own cries ignored.

thenVsNow.then: The principle of reciprocity in prayer: those who closed their ears to the suffering of others will find that God closes His ears to them.
thenVsNow.now: The principle of reciprocity in prayer: those who closed their ears to the suffering of others will find that God closes His ears to them.

verses.text: Yahweh says concerning the prophets who lead my people astray; for those who feed their teeth, they proclaim, “Peace!” and whoever doesn’t provide for their mouths, they prepare war against him:
verses.explainer: God indicts the false prophets who lead people astray: when they are well-fed, they proclaim 'Peace!' but when someone fails to feed them, they declare war. Their prophecies are for sale -- shaped by who pays them.

verses.text:
“Therefore night is over you, with no vision,
and it is dark to you, that you may not divine;
and the sun will go down on the prophets,
and the day will be black over them.
verses.explainer: Therefore the false prophets will experience night without vision, darkness without divination. The sun will set on them. God will take away their ability to prophesy -- the ultimate professional humiliation.

verses.text:
The seers shall be disappointed,
and the diviners confounded.
Yes, they shall all cover their lips;
for there is no answer from God.”
verses.explainer: The seers will be ashamed, the diviners disgraced. They will all cover their mouths because there is no answer from God. The prophets-for-hire will be exposed as frauds when God falls silent and their predictions fail.

verses.text:
But as for me, I am full of power by Yahweh’s Spirit,
and of judgment, and of might,
to declare to Jacob his disobedience,
and to Israel his sin.
verses.explainer: In contrast, Micah declares: I am filled with power by Yahweh's Spirit, with justice and might, to declare to Jacob his transgression. The true prophet's authority comes from God's Spirit, not from human payment or approval.

thenVsNow.then: The contrast between prophets who speak for pay and prophets who speak from God's Spirit raises the question: does financial incentive shape spiritual messaging? Micah insists that true authority comes only from divine calling.
thenVsNow.now: The contrast between prophets who speak for pay and prophets who speak from God's Spirit raises the question: does financial incentive shape spiritual messaging? Micah insists that true authority comes only from divine calling.

verses.text:
Please listen to this, you heads of the house of Jacob,
and rulers of the house of Israel,
who abhor justice,
and pervert all equity.
verses.explainer: Micah returns to addressing the rulers: you heads of Jacob and rulers of Israel who abhor justice and twist everything that is right. The leadership has inverted the moral order -- making the straight crooked.

verses.text:
They build up Zion with blood,
and Jerusalem with iniquity.
verses.explainer: They build Zion with bloodshed and Jerusalem with injustice. The very construction of the capital is stained with violence. The city's physical structures are built on the suffering of the exploited.

thenVsNow.then: Every prosperous system built on exploitation carries the blood of those who were crushed to build it. Micah insists that no amount of architectural glory justifies the injustice behind it.
thenVsNow.now: Every prosperous system built on exploitation carries the blood of those who were crushed to build it. Micah insists that no amount of architectural glory justifies the injustice behind it.

verses.text:
Her leaders judge for bribes,
and her priests teach for a price,
and her prophets of it tell fortunes for money:
yet they lean on Yahweh, and say,
“Isn’t Yahweh among us?
No disaster will come on us.”
verses.explainer: The leaders judge for bribes, the priests teach for payment, the prophets divine for money -- yet they all lean on God and say 'Isn't Yahweh among us? No disaster will come upon us.' They combine corruption with false confidence in God's protection.

verses.text:
Therefore Zion for your sake will be plowed like a field,
and Jerusalem will become heaps of rubble,
and the mountain of the temple like the high places of a forest.
verses.explainer: Therefore -- because of the leaders -- Zion will be plowed like a field, Jerusalem will become a heap of rubble, and the temple mount will be overgrown like a forest. This shocking prophecy, quoted a century later (Jeremiah 26:18), predicted the temple's destruction.

crossReferences.reference: Jeremiah 26:18 -- Elders quote this verse from Micah to save Jeremiah from execution: 'Micah of Moresheth prophesied this, and Hezekiah did not put him to death'
crossReferences.connection: Jeremiah 26:18 -- Elders quote this verse from Micah to save Jeremiah from execution: 'Micah of Moresheth prophesied this, and Hezekiah did not put him to death'

explainer.summary: The famous vision of the last days: nations streaming to God's mountain, swords beaten into plowshares, everyone under their own vine and fig tree. But first, Zion must go through exile to Babylon before rescue comes.

wordStudies.significance: The transformation of weapons into farming tools is the quintessential image of peace: military technology repurposed for food production.

verses.text:
But in the latter days,
it will happen that the mountain of Yahweh’s temple will be established on the top of the mountains,
and it will be exalted above the hills;
and peoples will stream to it.
verses.explainer: After the devastating judgment of 3:12, Micah pivots to a vision of the distant future: the mountain of God's temple will be elevated above all mountains, and nations will stream to it. Destruction gives way to exaltation.

crossReferences.reference: Isaiah 2:2-4 -- Nearly identical to Micah 4:1-3; these two prophets, contemporaries, share the same vision of universal peace
crossReferences.connection: Isaiah 2:2-4 -- Nearly identical to Micah 4:1-3; these two prophets, contemporaries, share the same vision of universal peace

verses.text:
Many nations will go and say,
“Come, and let us go up to the mountain of Yahweh,
and to the house of the God of Jacob;
and he will teach us of his ways,
and we will walk in his paths.”
For the law will go out of Zion,
and Yahweh’s word from Jerusalem;
verses.explainer: Many nations will come saying: 'Let us go up to the mountain of Yahweh!' He will teach us His ways and we will walk in His paths. God's instruction (Torah) will go out from Zion and His word from Jerusalem -- the city will become a center of universal teaching.

verses.text:
and he will judge between many peoples,
and will decide concerning strong nations afar off.
They will beat their swords into plowshares,
and their spears into pruning hooks.
Nation will not lift up sword against nation,
neither will they learn war any more.
verses.explainer: God will judge between nations and arbitrate for powerful peoples far and wide. They will beat swords into plowshares and spears into pruning hooks. Nation will not lift sword against nation, nor train for war anymore. The most famous peace vision in Scripture.

thenVsNow.then: This vision of universal disarmament has inspired peace movements for centuries. The United Nations building in New York has this verse inscribed on its wall, testifying to humanity's longing for its fulfillment.
thenVsNow.now: This vision of universal disarmament has inspired peace movements for centuries. The United Nations building in New York has this verse inscribed on its wall, testifying to humanity's longing for its fulfillment.

verses.explainer: Every person will sit under their own vine and fig tree, with no one to make them afraid. This image of personal security and agricultural peace represents the complete opposite of the land-grabbing condemned in chapter 2.

verses.text:
Indeed all the nations may walk in the name of their gods;
but we will walk in the name of Yahweh our God forever and ever.
verses.explainer: A statement of committed faith: though all nations walk in the name of their gods, we will walk in the name of Yahweh our God forever. Israel's loyalty to Yahweh persists regardless of what the surrounding nations do.

verses.text:
“In that day,” says Yahweh,
“I will assemble that which is lame,
and I will gather that which is driven away,
and that which I have afflicted;
verses.explainer: God will gather the lame, collect the scattered, and assemble those He has afflicted. The very people broken by judgment will become the foundation of God's restored kingdom.

verses.text:
and I will make that which was lame a remnant,
and that which was cast far off a strong nation:
and Yahweh will reign over them on Mount Zion from then on, even forever.”
verses.explainer: The lame will become a remnant, the cast-off a strong nation. Yahweh will reign over them from Mount Zion from now to forever. God's kingdom is built from the broken and discarded, not from the powerful.

thenVsNow.then: God's preference for building His kingdom from broken people rather than powerful ones is a consistent biblical theme -- anticipating Jesus's teaching that the last shall be first.
thenVsNow.now: God's preference for building His kingdom from broken people rather than powerful ones is a consistent biblical theme -- anticipating Jesus's teaching that the last shall be first.

verses.text:
You, tower of the flock, the hill of the daughter of Zion,
to you it will come,
yes, the former dominion will come,
the kingdom of the daughter of Jerusalem.
verses.explainer: The 'tower of the flock' (Migdal Eder) near Bethlehem will see the former dominion restored -- the kingdom of the daughter of Jerusalem will return. The shepherd's tower becomes a symbol of restored royal authority.

verses.text:
Now why do you cry out aloud?
Is there no king in you?
Has your counselor perished,
that pains have taken hold of you as of a woman in travail?
verses.explainer: Why do you cry aloud? Is there no king among you? Has your counselor perished? The nation writhes in pain like a woman in labor because it has lost its leadership. The rhetorical questions expose their spiritual bankruptcy.

verses.text:
Be in pain, and labor to give birth, daughter of Zion,
like a woman in travail;
for now you will go out of the city,
and will dwell in the field,
and will come even to Babylon.
There you will be rescued.
There Yahweh will redeem you from the hand of your enemies.
verses.explainer: Labor and deliver, daughter of Zion, like a woman giving birth. You will leave the city and live in the open field, and go to Babylon -- but there God will redeem you from your enemies. Exile is described as birth pangs leading to deliverance.

verses.text:
Now many nations have assembled against you, that say,
“Let her be defiled,
and let our eye gloat over Zion.”
verses.explainer: Many nations have assembled against Zion, saying: 'Let her be defiled! Let our eyes gloat over her!' The enemies gather, confident in their numbers and eager to see Jerusalem humiliated.
But the nations do not understand God's plan. He has gathered them like sheaves to the threshing floor -- they think they are conquering, but God has assembled them for their own judgment.

thenVsNow.then: What appears to be the triumph of enemies may actually be God gathering them for judgment. Appearances can be the opposite of reality in God's larger plan.
thenVsNow.now: What appears to be the triumph of enemies may actually be God gathering them for judgment. Appearances can be the opposite of reality in God's larger plan.

verses.explainer: Arise and thresh, daughter of Zion! God will give her iron horns and bronze hooves to crush the nations. The wealth they plundered will be devoted to Yahweh. The oppressed become the instrument of divine justice.

explainer.summary: The ruler of Israel will come from Bethlehem -- small among clans but from ancient origins. He will shepherd his flock in God's strength. The remnant of Jacob will be both dew (blessing) and lion (strength) among the nations.

wordStudies.significance: The coming ruler from Bethlehem has 'origins from of old, from ancient days' -- language suggesting pre-existence or at minimum an ancient, divinely established dynasty.

verses.text:
Now you shall gather yourself in troops,
daughter of troops.
He has laid siege against us.
They will strike the judge of Israel with a rod on the cheek.
verses.explainer: Now the daughter of troops prepares for siege. The judge of Israel is struck on the cheek -- a gesture of extreme insult. Before the messianic hope of verse 2, Micah acknowledges the present humiliation of Israel's leadership.

verses.text:
But you, Bethlehem Ephrathah,
being small among the clans of Judah,
out of you one will come out to me that is to be ruler in Israel;
whose goings out are from of old, from ancient times.
verses.explainer: The most famous verse in Micah: the ruler of Israel will come from Bethlehem Ephrathah -- too small to be among Judah's clans, yet from it will come the one whose origins are 'from of old, from ancient days.' This is the prophecy that guided the Magi to the birthplace of Jesus.

crossReferences.reference: Matthew 2:1-6 -- The chief priests quote this verse when Herod asks where the Messiah would be born
crossReferences.connection: Matthew 2:1-6 -- The chief priests quote this verse when Herod asks where the Messiah would be born

crossReferences.reference: John 7:42 -- 'Doesn't Scripture say that the Messiah will come from David's descendants and from Bethlehem?'
crossReferences.connection: John 7:42 -- 'Doesn't Scripture say that the Messiah will come from David's descendants and from Bethlehem?'

crossReferences.reference: Luke 2:4-7 -- Jesus born in Bethlehem, fulfilling Micah's prophecy
crossReferences.connection: Luke 2:4-7 -- Jesus born in Bethlehem, fulfilling Micah's prophecy

thenVsNow.then: God's choice of Bethlehem -- too small to be significant -- over Jerusalem embodies the entire theology of Micah: God works through the humble and small, not the powerful and prestigious.
thenVsNow.now: God's choice of Bethlehem -- too small to be significant -- over Jerusalem embodies the entire theology of Micah: God works through the humble and small, not the powerful and prestigious.

verses.text:
Therefore he will abandon them until the time that she who is in labor gives birth.
Then the rest of his brothers will return to the children of Israel.
verses.explainer: God will abandon Israel until the woman in labor gives birth. Then the rest of His people will return to the Israelites. The period of abandonment has a limit -- it ends when the promised ruler is born.

verses.text:
He shall stand, and shall shepherd in the strength of Yahweh,
in the majesty of the name of Yahweh his God:
and they will live, for then he will be great to the ends of the earth.
verses.explainer: The ruler will stand and shepherd his flock in the strength of Yahweh, in the majesty of God's name. His people will live securely because his greatness will reach to the ends of the earth. A shepherd-king whose authority is global.

annotations.word: shepherd in the strength of Yahweh

verses.text:
He will be our peace when Assyria invades our land,
and when he marches through our fortresses,
then we will raise against him seven shepherds,
and eight leaders of men.
verses.explainer: This ruler will be Israel's peace. When Assyria invades, Israel will raise seven shepherds and eight leaders against the threat. The coming king brings peace not through passivity but through capable leadership.

verses.text:
They will rule the land of Assyria with the sword,
and the land of Nimrod in its gates.
He will deliver us from the Assyrian,
when he invades our land,
and when he marches within our border.
verses.explainer: These leaders will rule even Assyria and Nimrod's land with the sword. The messianic king's authority extends over Israel's greatest enemy. What once threatened Israel will come under Israel's authority.

verses.text:
The remnant of Jacob will be among many peoples,
like dew from Yahweh,
like showers on the grass,
that don’t wait for man,
nor wait for the sons of men.
verses.explainer: The remnant of Jacob will be like dew from Yahweh among many peoples -- like gentle rain on grass that does not wait for human permission. Israel's presence among the nations will be a blessing, as naturally refreshing as morning dew.

verses.text:
The remnant of Jacob will be among the nations,
among many peoples,
like a lion among the animals of the forest,
like a young lion among the flocks of sheep;
who, if he goes through, treads down and tears in pieces,
and there is no one to deliver.
verses.explainer: The remnant of Jacob will also be like a lion among forest animals, like a young lion among flocks of sheep. Israel will be both gentle (dew, v.7) and powerful (lion, v.8) among the nations -- a dual nature.

verses.text:
Let your hand be lifted up above your adversaries,
and let all of your enemies be cut off.
verses.explainer: Your hand will be lifted in triumph over your adversaries, and all your enemies will be destroyed. The restored Israel will have complete victory over those who once oppressed them.

verses.text:
“It will happen in that day”, says Yahweh,
“that I will cut off your horses out from among you,
and will destroy your chariots.
verses.explainer: On that day, God will cut off Israel's horses and destroy their chariots. The instruments of military power will be removed because Israel will no longer need them -- God Himself will be their protection.

thenVsNow.then: God removes the things Israel trusted instead of Him. Military might, fortifications, and occult practices all represent self-reliance that competes with faith in God.
thenVsNow.now: God removes the things Israel trusted instead of Him. Military might, fortifications, and occult practices all represent self-reliance that competes with faith in God.

verses.text:
I will cut off the cities of your land,
and will tear down all your strongholds.
verses.explainer: God will destroy the fortified cities and tear down every stronghold. Israel's security will come from God alone, not from walls and military installations.

verses.text:
I will destroy witchcraft from your hand;
and you shall have no soothsayers.
verses.explainer: God will remove sorcery and soothsayers from Israel's midst. All forms of occult practice -- attempts to control the future apart from God -- will be eliminated.

verses.text:
I will cut off your engraved images and your pillars out from among you;
and you shall no more worship the work of your hands.
verses.explainer: God will destroy carved images and sacred pillars. Israel will no longer worship the work of their own hands. Idolatry -- worshiping human creations -- will be permanently abolished.

verses.text:
I will uproot your Asherah poles out from among you;
and I will destroy your cities.
verses.explainer: God will uproot the Asherah poles and destroy the cities associated with pagan worship. Every structure and symbol of false religion will be removed from the restored Israel.

verses.text:
I will execute vengeance in anger,
and wrath on the nations that didn’t listen.”
verses.explainer: God will execute vengeance in anger and wrath on the nations that refused to obey. While Israel is purified, the disobedient nations face judgment. God's justice is comprehensive -- none escape accountability.

explainer.summary: God brings a legal case against Israel: What have I done to you? How have I burdened you? He recounts His saving acts. Israel asks what offerings God wants -- thousands of rams? Rivers of oil? The answer is the most concise ethical summary in Scripture: do justice, love mercy, walk humbly with your God.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Steadfast love, loyal kindness, covenant faithfuln
wordStudies.meaning: Steadfast love, loyal kindness, covenant faithfulness
wordStudies.significance: The command to 'love chesed' means more than occasional kindness -- it means being passionately committed to loyal, faithful love as a way of life.

verses.text:
Listen now to what Yahweh says:
“Arise, plead your case before the mountains,
and let the hills hear what you have to say.
verses.explainer: God calls Israel to a cosmic courtroom: 'Arise, plead your case before the mountains!' The mountains and hills are summoned as witnesses to the dispute between God and His people. Creation itself will judge who is right.

annotations.word: plead your case before the mountains

verses.explainer: The mountains and enduring foundations of the earth are called to hear God's legal case against His people. These permanent features of creation were present when the covenant was made and can testify to its terms.

verses.text:
My people, what have I done to you?
How have I burdened you?
Answer me!
verses.explainer: God's opening plea is heartbreaking: 'My people, what have I done to you? How have I burdened you? Answer me!' God is not angry but bewildered -- what did He do wrong? The accused becomes the accuser's accuser.

thenVsNow.then: God's question 'What have I done to you?' reverses the expected dynamic. Instead of a distant judge demanding compliance, God is a wounded partner asking what went wrong in the relationship.
thenVsNow.now: God's question 'What have I done to you?' reverses the expected dynamic. Instead of a distant judge demanding compliance, God is a wounded partner asking what went wrong in the relationship.

verses.text:
For I brought you up out of the land of Egypt,
and redeemed you out of the house of bondage.
I sent before you Moses, Aaron, and Miriam.
verses.explainer: God presents His case: I brought you out of Egypt, redeemed you from slavery, and sent Moses, Aaron, and Miriam to lead you. The Exodus is Exhibit A in God's defense -- undeniable evidence of His faithfulness.

verses.text:
My people, remember now what Balak king of Moab devised,
and what Balaam the son of Beor answered him from Shittim to Gilgal,
that you may know the righteous acts of Yahweh.”
verses.explainer: Remember what Balak of Moab plotted and how Balaam son of Beor answered him. Remember the journey from Shittim to Gilgal. God asks Israel to recall how He protected them even from supernatural threats and brought them safely into the Promised Land.

crossReferences.reference: Numbers 22-24 -- Balak hired Balaam to curse Israel, but God turned the curses into blessings
crossReferences.connection: Numbers 22-24 -- Balak hired Balaam to curse Israel, but God turned the curses into blessings

verses.text:
How shall I come before Yahweh,
and bow myself before the exalted God?
Shall I come before him with burnt offerings,
with calves a year old?
verses.explainer: The people ask what they should bring to God: burnt offerings? Calves a year old? Thousands of rams? Rivers of olive oil? Should I sacrifice my firstborn child? The escalation from ordinary to extreme reveals their misunderstanding of what God wants.

verses.text:
Will Yahweh be pleased with thousands of rams?
With tens of thousands of rivers of oil?
Shall I give my firstborn for my disobedience?
The fruit of my body for the sin of my soul?
verses.explainer: Will Yahweh be pleased with thousands of rams or ten thousand rivers of oil? The absurd quantities reveal the futility of trying to buy God's favor with ritual. The question escalates to the ultimate horror: shall I give my firstborn child for my sin?

verses.text:
He has shown you, O man, what is good.
What does Yahweh require of you, but to act justly,
to love mercy, and to walk humbly with your God?
verses.explainer: The most beloved verse in Micah and one of the greatest ethical summaries in all of Scripture: He has told you, O mortal, what is good -- and what does Yahweh require of you but to do justice, love mercy, and walk humbly with your God?

crossReferences.reference: Deuteronomy 10:12 -- 'What does Yahweh your God require of you? To fear Yahweh, to walk in all his ways, to love him'
crossReferences.connection: Deuteronomy 10:12 -- 'What does Yahweh your God require of you? To fear Yahweh, to walk in all his ways, to love him'

crossReferences.reference: Matthew 23:23 -- Jesus condemns neglecting 'the more important matters of the law -- justice, mercy and faithfulness'
crossReferences.connection: Matthew 23:23 -- Jesus condemns neglecting 'the more important matters of the law -- justice, mercy and faithfulness'

crossReferences.reference: James 1:27 -- 'Religion that God our Father accepts as pure and faultless is this: to look after orphans and widows'
crossReferences.connection: James 1:27 -- 'Religion that God our Father accepts as pure and faultless is this: to look after orphans and widows'

thenVsNow.then: This verse distills the entire prophetic tradition into three commands that anyone can understand and practice. It cuts through religious complexity to the heart of what God actually wants: just behavior, merciful character, and humble relationship.
thenVsNow.now: This verse distills the entire prophetic tradition into three commands that anyone can understand and practice. It cuts through religious complexity to the heart of what God actually wants: just behavior, merciful character, and humble relationship.

verses.text:
Yahweh’s voice calls to the city,
and wisdom sees your name:
“Listen to the rod,
and he who appointed it.
verses.explainer: Yahweh's voice calls to the city with a warning. Wisdom recognizes God's name -- those who are wise will heed the coming rebuke. The 'rod' of discipline has been appointed, and the wise will listen to it.

verses.text:
Are there yet treasures of wickedness in the house of the wicked,
and a short ephah that is accursed?
verses.explainer: God asks: are there still treasures gained by wickedness in the house of the wicked? Are merchants still using the 'short ephah' -- a deliberately undersized measure to cheat customers? God is auditing the nation's business practices.

verses.text:
Shall I be pure with dishonest scales,
and with a bag of deceitful weights?
verses.explainer: Can God declare someone pure who uses dishonest scales and deceitful weights? The answer is obviously no. Fraudulent business practices disqualify one from standing in God's favor, no matter how religious one appears.

crossReferences.reference: Proverbs 11:1 -- 'Dishonest scales are an abomination to Yahweh, but a full weight is his delight'
crossReferences.connection: Proverbs 11:1 -- 'Dishonest scales are an abomination to Yahweh, but a full weight is his delight'

verses.text:
Her rich men are full of violence,
her inhabitants speak lies,
and their tongue is deceitful in their speech.
verses.explainer: The city's wealthy are full of violence, its inhabitants speak lies, and their tongues are deceitful. The corruption is not limited to the marketplace -- it pervades all speech and social interaction.

verses.text:
Therefore I also have struck you with a grievous wound.
I have made you desolate because of your sins.
verses.explainer: Therefore God has struck them with devastating blows, making them desolate because of their sins. The judgment is already underway -- God's discipline has begun.

verses.text:
You shall eat, but not be satisfied.
Your humiliation will be within you.
You will store up, but not save;
and that which you save I will give up to the sword.
verses.explainer: You will eat but never be satisfied -- emptiness will remain inside you. You will store up but save nothing. What you do save, God will give to the sword. All accumulation becomes futile under God's judgment.

You will sow but not reap, press olives but not use the oil, crush grapes but not drink the wine. Every productive activity will fail to produce its expected result. The covenant curses of Deuteronomy 28 are activated.

crossReferences.reference: Deuteronomy 28:38-40 -- Nearly identical futility curses: 'You will sow much seed but harvest little... you will have olive trees but not use the oil'
crossReferences.connection: Deuteronomy 28:38-40 -- Nearly identical futility curses: 'You will sow much seed but harvest little... you will have olive trees but not use the oil'

verses.text:
For the statutes of Omri are kept,
and all the works of Ahab’s house.
You walk in their counsels,
that I may make you a ruin,
and her inhabitants a hissing;
And you will bear the reproach of my people.”
verses.explainer: The root cause: Israel has followed the statutes of Omri and the practices of Ahab's house. These kings institutionalized Baal worship in northern Israel. Judah has adopted the north's corruptions, ensuring its own desolation.

explainer.summary: Micah laments the moral decay of society -- no faithful person remains, everyone lies in wait for blood. But the book ends with stunning hope: God will again have compassion, tread iniquities underfoot, and cast all sins into the depths of the sea. Who is a God like you, who pardons sin?

wordStudies.transliteration: Who is a God like you?
wordStudies.translatedAs: A wordplay on Micah's own name: 'Who is like God?'
wordStudies.meaning: A wordplay on Micah's own name: 'Who is like God?'
wordStudies.significance: The book ends with a question that echoes the prophet's own name. The answer: no one is like God in His willingness to forgive and restore.

verses.text:
Misery is mine!
Indeed, I am like one who gathers the summer fruits, as gleanings of the vineyard:
There is no cluster of grapes to eat.
My soul desires to eat the early fig.
verses.explainer: Micah laments: 'Misery is mine!' He is like someone searching for fruit after the harvest -- nothing remains. There are no righteous people left, no 'first-ripe fruit' to be found. The faithful have vanished.

verses.text:
The godly man has perished out of the earth,
and there is no one upright among men.
They all lie in wait for blood;
every man hunts his brother with a net.
verses.explainer: The godly have perished from the land, no upright person remains. Everyone lies in wait for blood, hunting their neighbors with nets. Society has become predatory -- each person stalking the other.

verses.text:
Their hands are on that which is evil to do it diligently.
The ruler and judge ask for a bribe;
and the powerful man dictates the evil desire of his soul.
Thus they conspire together.
verses.explainer: Hands are skilled at doing evil. The ruler demands bribes, the judge accepts them, and the powerful dictate what they want. Together they weave a conspiracy of corruption -- evil is done with professional efficiency.

verses.text:
The best of them is like a brier.
The most upright is worse than a thorn hedge.
The day of your watchmen,
even your visitation, has come;
now is the time of their confusion.
verses.explainer: The best of them is like a briar, the most upright worse than a thorn hedge. The day of judgment announced by the watchmen (prophets) has arrived, and now confusion reigns among the guilty.

verses.text:
Don’t trust in a neighbor.
Don’t put confidence in a friend.
With the woman lying in your embrace,
be careful of the words of your mouth!
verses.explainer: Trust no neighbor, put no confidence in a friend. Even guard the words you speak to the wife in your arms. The social fabric is so corrupted that the most intimate relationships cannot be trusted.

crossReferences.reference: Matthew 10:35-36 -- Jesus quotes Micah 7:6 when describing the divisions His message will create
crossReferences.connection: Matthew 10:35-36 -- Jesus quotes Micah 7:6 when describing the divisions His message will create

verses.text:
For the son dishonors the father,
the daughter rises up against her mother,
the daughter-in-law against her mother-in-law;
a man’s enemies are the men of his own house.
verses.explainer: The son dishonors his father, the daughter rises against her mother, the daughter-in-law against her mother-in-law. A person's enemies are the members of their own household. Family bonds have completely broken down.

crossReferences.reference: Matthew 10:35-36 -- Jesus quotes this verse directly: 'For I have come to turn a man against his father, a daughter against her mother'
crossReferences.connection: Matthew 10:35-36 -- Jesus quotes this verse directly: 'For I have come to turn a man against his father, a daughter against her mother'

verses.text:
But as for me, I will look to Yahweh.
I will wait for the God of my salvation.
My God will hear me.
verses.explainer: In contrast to the social breakdown, Micah declares personal resolve: 'But as for me, I will look to Yahweh. I will wait for the God of my salvation. My God will hear me.' When every human relationship fails, the relationship with God remains.

thenVsNow.then: When trust in human institutions and relationships collapses, the declaration 'as for me, I will look to Yahweh' becomes the anchor of hope. Personal faith sustains when social structures fail.
thenVsNow.now: When trust in human institutions and relationships collapses, the declaration 'as for me, I will look to Yahweh' becomes the anchor of hope. Personal faith sustains when social structures fail.

verses.text:
Don’t rejoice against me, my enemy.
When I fall, I will arise.
When I sit in darkness, Yahweh will be a light to me.
verses.explainer: A message to the enemy: don't rejoice over me. When I fall, I will rise. When I sit in darkness, Yahweh will be my light. Defeat is not permanent -- God's light penetrates even the deepest darkness of judgment.

verses.text:
I will bear the indignation of Yahweh,
because I have sinned against him,
until he pleads my case, and executes judgment for me.
He will bring me out to the light.
I will see his righteousness.
verses.explainer: Micah accepts God's discipline: 'I will bear Yahweh's indignation because I have sinned against Him.' But this acceptance is temporary -- God will eventually plead his case, execute justice, and bring him into the light.

verses.text:
Then my enemy will see it,
and shame will cover her who said to me,
where is Yahweh your God?
Then my enemy will see me and will cover her shame.
Now she will be trodden down like the mire of the streets.
verses.explainer: Then the enemy who taunted 'Where is Yahweh your God?' will see God's vindication and be covered in shame. The one who mocked Israel's faith will be trampled like mud in the streets.

verses.text:
A day to build your walls—
In that day, he will extend your boundary.
verses.explainer: A day is coming for rebuilding walls and extending borders. The time of exile and confinement will give way to expansion and restoration.

verses.text:
In that day they will come to you from Assyria and the cities of Egypt,
and from Egypt even to the River,
and from sea to sea,
and mountain to mountain.
verses.explainer: In that day, people will come from Assyria, Egypt, and from sea to sea, from mountain to mountain -- a universal gathering. The scattered will return from every direction and distance.

verses.text:
Yet the land will be desolate because of those who dwell therein,
for the fruit of their doings.
verses.explainer: But before restoration, the land will be desolate because of the sins of its inhabitants. The sequence is clear: sin leads to desolation, then desolation gives way to restoration. Judgment is the path through which renewal comes.

verses.text:
Shepherd your people with your staff,
the flock of your heritage,
who dwell by themselves in a forest,
in the middle of fertile pasture land, let them feed;
in Bashan and Gilead, as in the days of old.
verses.explainer: A prayer: Shepherd your people with your staff, the flock of your heritage. Let them graze in Bashan and Gilead as in ancient days. Micah asks God to resume His role as Israel's shepherd, restoring them to their ancestral pastures.

verses.text:
“As in the days of your coming out of the land of Egypt,
I will show them marvelous things.”
verses.explainer: God responds to the prayer: 'As in the days when you came out of Egypt, I will show them marvelous things.' God promises a second Exodus -- a future deliverance as miraculous as the original.

verses.text:
The nations will see and be ashamed of all their might.
They will lay their hand on their mouth.
Their ears will be deaf.
verses.explainer: The nations will see God's mighty acts and be ashamed of their own strength. They will cover their mouths in stunned silence and stop their ears -- overwhelmed by what God has done for Israel.

verses.text:
They will lick the dust like a serpent.
Like crawling things of the earth they shall come trembling out of their dens.
They will come with fear to Yahweh our God,
and will be afraid because of you.
verses.explainer: The nations will lick dust like serpents, crawling out of their strongholds in terror. They will come trembling to Yahweh and be afraid before Israel's God. The proud nations are reduced to groveling submission.

verses.text:
Who is a God like you, who pardons iniquity,
and passes over the disobedience of the remnant of his heritage?
He doesn’t retain his anger forever,
because he delights in loving kindness.
verses.explainer: Who is a God like you, who pardons sin and forgives the transgression of the remnant of His inheritance? You do not stay angry forever but delight in showing mercy. A wordplay on Micah's own name ('Who is like God?') closes the book.

crossReferences.reference: Exodus 15:11 -- 'Who is like you, Yahweh, among the gods?'
crossReferences.connection: Exodus 15:11 -- 'Who is like you, Yahweh, among the gods?'

crossReferences.reference: Psalm 103:8-12 -- 'Yahweh is compassionate and gracious, slow to anger, abounding in love'
crossReferences.connection: Psalm 103:8-12 -- 'Yahweh is compassionate and gracious, slow to anger, abounding in love'

verses.explainer: God will again have compassion, tread our iniquities underfoot, and hurl all our sins into the depths of the sea. The image of sins cast into the ocean -- irretrievable, unreachable, gone forever -- is one of the most powerful forgiveness images in Scripture.

crossReferences.reference: Psalm 103:12 -- 'As far as the east is from the west, so far has he removed our transgressions from us'
crossReferences.connection: Psalm 103:12 -- 'As far as the east is from the west, so far has he removed our transgressions from us'

crossReferences.reference: Isaiah 43:25 -- 'I, even I, am he who blots out your transgressions... and remembers your sins no more'
crossReferences.connection: Isaiah 43:25 -- 'I, even I, am he who blots out your transgressions... and remembers your sins no more'

thenVsNow.then: The image of sins thrown into the depths of the sea has comforted believers for millennia. It teaches that God's forgiveness is not partial or conditional but total and permanent -- sins are not just pardoned but destroyed.
thenVsNow.now: The image of sins thrown into the depths of the sea has comforted believers for millennia. It teaches that God's forgiveness is not partial or conditional but total and permanent -- sins are not just pardoned but destroyed.

verses.text:
You will give truth to Jacob,
and mercy to Abraham,
as you have sworn to our fathers from the days of old.
verses.explainer: The closing verse appeals to God's ancient promises: You will give truth to Jacob and mercy to Abraham, as you swore to our ancestors in the days of old. Micah ends where he began -- with God's covenant faithfulness. The promises made to Abraham still stand.

crossReferences.reference: Genesis 12:1-3 -- God's original covenant promises to Abraham
crossReferences.connection: Genesis 12:1-3 -- God's original covenant promises to Abraham

crossReferences.reference: Luke 1:54-55 -- Mary's Magnificat: 'He has helped his servant Israel, remembering to be merciful to Abraham and his descendants forever'
crossReferences.connection: Luke 1:54-55 -- Mary's Magnificat: 'He has helped his servant Israel, remembering to be merciful to Abraham and his descendants forever'
